segments.s457-g25: ببخشید is used as a past/transitive verb from بخشیدن; here it functions as ‘gave/handed over’ or ‘distributed’ rather than the usual sense ‘forgave’. چیزی که is a noun + relative clause 'something that…'; بد بر سپاه is an adjectival phrase 'bad/ill upon the army' (بد = harmful/ill, بر = upon/against, سپاه = army), so the clause says they gave/handed over something that was harmful to the army.
segments.s457-g26: The repeated ز is the poetic preposition 'from' listing sources or items; the nouns name martial equipment or mounts. اسپ (steed/horse), رمح (spear/lance), تیغ (sword) and کلاه (literally 'hat' but here used as battle-helm). The conjunction و simply links the items.
segments.s457-g27: ز لشکر is a partitive 'from the army'; یکی functions as 'a certain' (not strictly the numeral one) modifying مرزبان; برگزید is the simple past 'he chose/appointed' placed after its object, a normal order in classical narrative.
segments.s457-g28: که introduces a relative clause modifying مرزبان. گفتار ایشان = 'their speech/words'. بداند is the (present/subjunctive/optative) form of دانستن used here with an ability/expectation sense 'may/should know' or 'be able to know'; شنید is the past 'he heard' and here together they express that the appointed man would hear and understand their words (an older/clipped classical sequence: 'who would know by hearing').
segments.s457-g29: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent'. تا here introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'. باژ is a cry/noise (a rallying or alarm cry) and خواهد is the auxiliary/future 'will', so باژ خواهد ز دشت means 'a cry will go forth from the plain'.
segments.s457-g30: ازان سال و آن سال = 'from that year and that year' (classical phrasing for successive years); کاندر is an archaic fused preposition/relative 'in which/that in', and گذشت is the past 'passed'. Together they indicate the passing of those years (i.e., 'as those years passed').
segments.s457-g9: زمین (subject) + آن سپه را (definite object marked by را) precedes the verb; همی is an archaic/progressive/emphatic particle placed before the verb to give a continuous or emphatic flavour, and برنتافت is a past negative verb meaning ‘would not endure/accept’. The particle را marks آن سپه as the definite object of برنتافت.
segments.s457-g10: بران here functions as بر + آن ‘on/at that’ (locative), followed by بوم ‘land/plain’. کس with the negative verb نیافت yields the idiom ‘no one…found’; جای رفتن is a nominal phrase (‘a place for going’) where رفتن is the infinitive used as complement (i.e. ‘a place to go’). The verb نیافت is past negative ‘did not find’.
segments.s457-g11: ز is the causal/prepositional 'from/because of'; باران is used figuratively as ‘a shower’ (i.e. a heavy falling of items); ژوپین is a gambeson/padded jacket (here the image is a ‘rain’ of armor-piercing blows or pieces), and the second phrase باران تیر parallels the first (‘and a shower of arrows’). The coordination links two figurative ‘rains’ as causes.
segments.s457-g12: زمین شد is a subject + past copula/verb ‘the earth became’; ز خون is an ablative/causal phrase ‘from/because of blood’ modifying شد; چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and یکی آبگیر is a noun phrase ‘a single pond’ (یکی modifying آبگیر). Overall: ‘the earth became, from blood, like one pond.’
segments.s457-g13: خروشی (a roar/clamour) is placed before the verb for poetic effect; برآمد is the past verb ‘arose/issued forth’; ز هر پهلوی is a prepositional phrase ‘from every flank/side’ giving the source of the sound. Word order here emphasizes the sound (noun first).
segments.s457-g14: تلی کشته is a noun phrase ‘a heap/pile of slain’ (تلی modifying کشته); دیدند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they saw’; بر هر سوی is a locative phrase ‘on every side’. The clause states the observers’ perception: ‘they saw heaps of the slain on every side.’
segments.s457-g15: سه روز و سه شب is a time expression ‘three days and three nights’. زین نشان here is a compact/poetic phrase: نشان ‘sign/mark’ is used to refer back to the foregoing spectacle; زین functions as a poetic ‘ز این’ (literally ‘from/with this’) — an archaic usage not listed in the given senses — and جنگ بود is the existential past ‘there was war/fighting’. The whole means ‘for three days and three nights there was war like this’.
segments.s457-g16: تو گفتی is the reporting clause ‘you said’ (گفتی here is past 2nd-person ‘you said’ — the provided noun senses do not match this verbal use). بریشان is an archaic/dialectal dative meaning ‘for them’ (not directly covered by the listed senses for بر), and جهان تنگ بود is the predicative clause ‘the world was narrow’ (an idiom meaning the world was too small for them / they had no room). Together: ‘you said the world was narrow for them.’
segments.s457-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed classical phrasing meaning “so/so bad that” (چنان = “such/so”, بد = “bad/terrible”) and introduces a result clause with که. از + تازیان marks the source/party “from the Arabs.” صدهزار is the numeric noun phrase “one hundred thousand” following the source phrase.
segments.s457-g2: نبرده here is a negative perfect/past-form (ن- + برده) used without an expressed subject; with the preceding از تازیان صدهزار it is best read as passive/impersonal: “were not taken” (i.e. not even that many were taken from the Arabs). سواران نیزه‌گزار is a compound noun phrase “spear-bearing horsemen” (سواران = horsemen/riders; نیزه‌گزار = ‘spear-bearer’), serving as the logical patient/quantity referred to by نبرده.
segments.s457-g3: برفتند is the past plural narrative “they went/they departed.” و links clauses. سالار ایشان is a possessive construction where سالار (commander) is qualified by ایشان (“their” — classical polite/third-person form), so “their commander.” شعیب is the proper name that identifies that commander.
segments.s457-g4: یکی here is an indefinite “one/a certain” (introducing an appositive). نامدار is an adjective “renowned/illustrious.” از نژاد is the standard preposition + noun “of the lineage/tribe,” and قتیب is a proper tribal/name label; the whole phrase apposes Shuaib: “a famed one of the Qatib line.”
segments.s457-g5: جهاندار ایران is a genitival phrase “the ruler/sovereign of Iran” (جهاندار = sovereign). سپاهی (here a noun) = “an army” or “a force,” and ببرد is the past narrative form of بردن meaning “took/led off” (subject + object + verb order: the ruler took an army).
segments.s457-g6: بگفتند is the narrative past “they said.” کان is an archaic/demonstrative pronoun here meaning “that (thing)” and takes را as the definite object marker. نشاید is a prescriptive/impersonal modal “it is not fitting/one should not,” and شمرد is the infinitive/root “to count/ reckon”; together کان را نشاید شمرد = “that should not be counted/ reckoned.”
segments.s457-g7: فراز آمدند is a verbal phrase meaning “came up/approached from above” (فراز used adverbially + آمدند “they came”). آن دو لشکر is “those two armies,” and بهم here means “to one another/against each other” indicating mutual approach — so: “those two armies came up against one another.”
segments.s457-g8: جهان شد is a simple copular construction “the world became.” ز is the poetic preposition “because of/from,” introducing the cause. پرخاشجویان is a plural noun “the aggressive/quarrelsome ones,” and دژم is a predicate adjective “grim/sullen” applied to the world: “the world became grim because of the quarrelsome aggressors.”
segments.s457-g17: چهارم here is an ordinal/adverbial phrase 'on the fourth (day)' placed before the clause to give the time. عرب is the (unsuffixed) plural subject 'the Arabs' and برگاشتند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they turned/returned' (برگشت + -ند). روی is the noun 'face' used idiomatically with برگشتن (روی برگشتن) to mean 'to turn back/withdraw'.
segments.s457-g18: به شب is a temporal prepositional phrase 'towards/into night' (here functioning as 'by night' or 'at night'). دشت پیکار is a noun phrase 'plain (دشت) of battle (پیکار)' and بگذاشتند is the past 3rd-person plural of گذاشتن with the verbal prefix ب-: here 'they set up/established' (i.e. 'they set the battle' or 'took up battle'). The verb comes at the end, reflecting the verb-final tendency of Persian clauses.
segments.s457-g19: شعیب is a proper name. اندران is an archaic locative/relational adverb 'among/therein' (i.e. 'there among them' or 'in that place'). رزمگه ‘battlefield’ is the locative noun. کشته plus the auxiliary شد forms a passive: کشته شد = 'was killed' (past passive), with شد acting as the auxiliary 'to be' here.
segments.s457-g20: عرب را uses را in a topical/emphatic function (an older usage where را marks the topic rather than a direct object). همه روز is an adverbial 'all day' modifying the action, and برگشته شد is a verbal phrase ('was/has turned back' or 'retreated') formed with the past participle برگشته plus auxiliary شد; word order places the topic before the temporal adverb and verb.
segments.s457-g21: بسی here is an older/poetic quantifier meaning 'many' or 'much' placed before the noun. اسپ is 'horse/steed' and تازی follows as a postpositive adjective specifying type ('Arabian' — i.e. Arabian horse). به زین is a locative/relational phrase 'on/at the saddle' (به + زین), and خدنگ 'dagger' names the weapon associated with the saddle.
segments.s457-g22: هم 'also' introduces additional instruments of fighting. از marks the instruments or means ('with'/'by' in older usage) and links to a series joined by و. The list (نیزه و تیغ و خفتان) names arms; جنگ at the end frames the whole phrase as pertaining to battle. Note the verb is omitted here (ellipsis) and is understood from surrounding clauses.
segments.s457-g23: ازان ('from that' / 'of those') introduces the group being spoken about (often 'of those who had gone' / 'of the departed'). رفتگان is a nominal form meaning 'the departed' or 'those who left/are gone'. ماند is the past intransitive 'remained/was left' and آنجا به جای is a locative phrase 'there, in place' (به جای = 'in the place/stead'). The sequence follows Persian order with the noun phrase before the verb and the locative phrase after it.
segments.s457-g24: به نزد is a compound prepositional expression 'to the presence of' or 'before'. جهاندار is a titulary noun 'sovereign' and پور is the patronymic element 'son (of)'; همای is a proper name (Humay). The whole phrase names the recipient/location: 'in the presence of the sovereign, son of Humay.'

sentences.s457-s13: They gave up/handed over something that proved ill for the army — from horse and from lance and from sword and helmet.
sentences.s457-s14: From the army he chose a certain marzban (frontier guardian) who would hear and know their speech.
sentences.s457-s15: He sent (them) so that a cry would go up from the plain, from that year and that year which passed.
sentences.s457-s5: The ground would not endure that host; on that plain no one could find a place to go.
sentences.s457-s6: From a shower of gambesons and a shower of arrows, the earth, stained with blood, became like a single pond.
sentences.s457-s7: A roar rose from every flank; heaps of the slain were seen on every side.
sentences.s457-s8: For three days and three nights there was such fighting; you said the world was too narrow for them.
sentences.s457-s1: So terrible that not even a hundred thousand spear-bearing horsemen were taken from the Arabs.
sentences.s457-s2: They departed, and their commander was Shuaib, a renowned man of the Qatib line.
sentences.s457-s3: The sovereign (ruler) of Iran took an army; they said that that ought not to be counted.
sentences.s457-s4: The two armies came up against one another; the world grew grim because of the quarrelsome aggressors.
sentences.s457-s9: On the fourth day the Arabs turned back; by night they set the battle on the plain.
sentences.s457-s10: Shuʿayb was slain there on the battlefield; the Arabs retreated all day.
sentences.s457-s11: Many Arabian steeds, daggers at their saddles; they also fought with spear and sword and other arms.
sentences.s457-s12: Of those departed, some remained there in their place, in the presence of the sovereign, son of Humay.
